narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your odds. For you specifically, it calculated a 50.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a risk high enough that the admissions team decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted outcomes. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your family income level, your LSAT score, and your Year 3 school decile ranking. Interestingly, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, nudging the model toward predicting you'd pass, as students from similar backgrounds sometimes outperform expectations. However, your LSAT score of 24—well below the average of about 37—had the strongest push toward failure, signaling to the model a notable gap in the standardized reasoning skills it associates with bar success. Your Year 3 decile of 2, compared to the typical 5.5, further reinforced that concern by highlighting relatively lower grades in your later undergraduate years. Compared to typical students who pass, where undergrad GPAs average 3.21 and Year 1 deciles hit around 5.7, your overall profile like your 2.4 GPA and Year 1 decile of 4 placed you below the median in key academic areas that the model weighs heavily. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority at 93%, so it didn't sway things much either way. This combination tipped the scales just over the edge into predicted failure territory. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience and potential as a Black man pursuing law. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly in future cycles.
